title: How to build energy resilience in an uncertain, contested world
gist: Atlantic Council Global Energy Forum experts assess the 2026 Strait of Hormuz closure crisis: over 1 billion barrels of Mideast Gulf oil supply already lost and the world's largest LNG export facility shut since March, yet physical energy systems have absorbed the shock with surprising resilience. The recurring warning is that markets and policymakers may be drawing the wrong, overconfident lesson from a still-manageable crisis, and that resilience (storage, diversified routes, the 'boring' grid middle) is becoming as important as efficiency.

themes.theme: Hormuz crisis: scale of supply loss and flow shifts

insights.claim: More than 1 billion barrels of Mideast Gulf oil supply have already been lost since the Strait of Hormuz disruption began, which Ben Cahill calls the largest supply disruption in history.
insights.speaker: Ben Cahill
insights.so_what: Quantifies the physical supply hole driving elevated oil prices; a >1bn bbl loss with uncertain restoration timing keeps a structural premium in crude and refined products.
insights.quote: Already, more than 1 billion barrels of Mideast Gulf oil supply have been lost.
insights.locator: The world has lost one billion barrels of oil so far

insights.claim: The world's largest LNG export facility has been shut in since March 2026 amid damage to oil and gas infrastructure across the Gulf region.
insights.speaker: Ben Cahill
insights.so_what: A prolonged outage at the single largest LNG exporter (Qatar) tightens global gas balances and supports spot LNG and US LNG cargo economics.
insights.quote: The world's largest liquefied natural gas (LNG) export facility has been shut in since March.
insights.locator: The world has lost one billion barrels of oil so far

insights.claim: Crude importers are replacing Middle Eastern barrels largely with Western hemisphere and Russian oil, but Asian refiners substituting for heavy and medium sour Gulf crudes face higher costs and lower efficiency because crude quality matters.
insights.speaker: Ben Cahill
insights.so_what: Watch refining margins and product spreads (e.g. jet fuel) as slate adjustments raise costs; quality mismatch is a hidden margin drag even when volumes are replaced.
insights.quote: Asian refiners can substitute for heavy and medium sour crudes from the Gulf, but these adjustments raise costs and reduce efficiency.
insights.locator: Crude flows are changing

insights.claim: US LNG cargo flexibility is facilitating market rebalancing via the spot market as Qatar's buyers seek alternatives, with Northeast Asian spot LNG prices holding a premium to European prices.
insights.speaker: Ben Cahill
insights.so_what: The NE Asia-over-Europe LNG spot premium is the live signal of where cargoes get pulled; favors US LNG exporters with destination-flexible volumes.
insights.quote: Spot prices in Northeast Asia remain at a premium to those in Europe.
insights.locator: LNG flows are also changing

insights.claim: Energy systems have shown surprising resilience through two global energy shocks in five years, but experts warn this resilience was not costless and may be tested past its limit as the Hormuz disruption continues.
insights.speaker: srothbardt
insights.so_what: The bull case for calm markets rests on absorbed-but-finite buffers; positioning that assumes automatic adaptation is exposed if buffers exhaust.
insights.locator: As war and the weaponization of energy drag on

insights.claim: The Hormuz stress was absorbed throughout the system by traders, refiners, infrastructure operators, and governments rather than being costless, raising the risk that policymakers and investors have become overly confident in markets' ability to adapt automatically to disruption.
insights.speaker: Philip Cornell
insights.so_what: Flags a consensus blind spot: low headline volatility hides distributed stress, so the wrong lesson (markets self-heal) could lead to underinvestment in resilience.
insights.quote: stress was absorbed throughout the system by traders, refiners, infrastructure operators, and governments. This raised broader questions about whether policymakers and investors have become overly confident
insights.locator: Products Under Pressure stress test panel

insights.claim: Resilience is increasingly becoming as important as efficiency in energy system design, elevating storage, interconnections, diversified supply routes, strategic reserves, and infrastructure redundancy.
insights.speaker: Philip Cornell
insights.so_what: A structural shift from efficiency-optimization to redundancy implies sustained capex into storage, interconnects, and reserves -- a multi-year demand theme.
insights.quote: there was broad agreement that resilience is increasingly becoming as important as efficiency
insights.locator: Looking ahead, the panel considered

insights.claim: Visible price signals diverged from underlying physical stress during the crisis, and a narrative centered on electrons distorted the still-critical importance of hydrocarbons.
insights.speaker: Philip Cornell
insights.so_what: Price calm can understate physical tightness; investors over-indexed on the electrification narrative may be mispricing continued hydrocarbon dependence.
insights.quote: a narrative based around electrons distorted the ongoing importance of hydrocarbons
insights.locator: Products Under Pressure stress test panel

themes.theme: European energy security after two crises

insights.claim: Europe is in better shape to withstand the current Hormuz shock than it was after Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, having learned lessons from its overreliance on a single imported gas source.
insights.speaker: Charles Hendry
insights.so_what: Reduced single-supplier exposure means Europe's gas exposure this cycle is more about price/route risk than physical cutoff; a relatively less fragile setup than 2022.
insights.quote: it is in better shape to withstand the shock than it was after Russia's invasion of Ukraine
insights.locator: Rethinking European energy policies

insights.claim: The new Vertical Corridor is bringing LNG into Greece and up through Romania, Bulgaria, and Moldova to Ukraine, cited as evidence of successful US-Europe energy cooperation on route diversification.
insights.speaker: Charles Hendry
insights.so_what: A concrete new LNG route into Central/Eastern Europe and Ukraine -- infrastructure that reroutes gas flows away from Russian dependence.
insights.quote: the remarkable success of the new Vertical Corridor—bringing liquefied natural gas (LNG) into Greece and up through Romania, Bulgaria, and Moldova to Ukraine
insights.locator: Rethinking European energy policies

insights.claim: EU policy is committed to 'sovereign' indigenous energy sources so Europe is never again held to ransom over imports, which doubles as a decarbonization driver for power systems.
insights.speaker: Charles Hendry
insights.so_what: The security-driven push to indigenous sources reinforces European domestic clean-power buildout as a policy floor independent of climate politics.
insights.quote: Europe should never again be held to ransom over its energy imports
insights.locator: Rethinking European energy policies

themes.theme: US Western hemisphere energy policy contradictions

insights.claim: The Trump administration's 'Trump Corollary to the Monroe doctrine' uses military force (Venezuela), financial support (Argentina), sanctions (Cuba), and tariff coercion (Canada, Mexico, Brazil, Colombia) to shift hemisphere energy revenue away from China and Russia toward the US.
insights.speaker: David Goldwyn
insights.so_what: A coherent lens for trading hemisphere energy political risk: each country faces a different US tool, with energy revenue realignment as the goal.
insights.quote: This Trump Corollary to the Monroe doctrine has utilized military force in the case of Venezuela, financial support in the case of Argentina, sanctions and isolation in the case of Cuba, and attempted coercion through tariff policy
insights.locator: The US policies that help and hinder

insights.claim: Venezuela could add 200,000 to 400,000 barrels a day of incremental oil supply over the next two years following US-prompted hydrocarbon and mining law reforms, though the framework still has weak arbitration provisions and excessive ministry discretion to cancel projects.
insights.speaker: David Goldwyn
insights.so_what: A concrete 200-400 kbd non-OPEC supply call on Venezuela; investability hinges on fixing arbitration and project-cancellation risk -- watch the reform trajectory.
insights.quote: Venezuela is trending upwards, with the potential to add 200,000 to 400,000 barrels a day in incremental supply growth in the next two years.
insights.locator: The US policies that help and hinder

insights.claim: US tariffs on steel and aluminum inputs raise the cost of building energy infrastructure in the US, Canada, and Mexico, and USMCA renewal uncertainty threatens the near-shoring agenda by potentially disrupting integrated continental oil, gas, and electricity markets.
insights.speaker: David Goldwyn
insights.so_what: Self-inflicted policy contradiction: tariffs and trade-deal uncertainty are headwinds to the very North American energy integration the administration wants -- a cost/risk overhang on infrastructure builds.
insights.quote: The cost of energy production in Canada and Mexico, and indeed the United States itself, is challenged by high tariffs on the steel and aluminum inputs required for building new energy infrastructure.
insights.locator: The US policies that help and hinder

insights.claim: Major Western hemisphere producers Brazil, Canada, Guyana, and Argentina were already on established supply-growth pathways before the Trump administration, and the most effective US policy would be to 'do no harm' by avoiding input tariffs and extending USMCA.
insights.speaker: David Goldwyn
insights.so_what: Frames hemisphere supply growth as policy-independent; the main risk to the bullish supply case is US policy own-goals rather than geology.
insights.locator: When it comes to promoting hemispheric energy security

themes.theme: Energy-tech innovation, IPOs, and deployment bottlenecks

insights.claim: Small modular nuclear reactor company X-energy raised more than one billion dollars and Fervo became the first advanced geothermal company to go public, both having benefited early from modest ARPA-E public investment targeting specific technology choke points.
insights.speaker: Lee Beck
insights.so_what: Historic clean-tech IPOs signal capital-market enthusiasm for SMRs and geothermal; ARPA-E early support is a leading indicator for which deep-tech energy names attract later VC/industry capital.
insights.quote: small modular nuclear reactor company X-energy raised more than one billion dollars, while Fervo became the first advanced geothermal company to go public
insights.locator: The decisions driving energy innovation

insights.claim: The 'boring but critical middle' of deployment -- grids, transmission, transformers, inverters, control systems -- may prove as decisive as innovation and increasingly sits at the intersection of industrial competitiveness and national security.
insights.speaker: David Livingston
insights.so_what: Points capital toward unglamorous grid hardware (transformers, transmission, controls) as the deployment bottleneck and a national-security-adjacent investment theme.
insights.quote: This "boring but critical middle," says Livingston, increasingly sits at the intersection of industrial competitiveness and national security.
insights.locator: Yet innovation alone is not enough

insights.claim: Businesses abandoned over $35 billion in clean energy projects in 2025 alone, underscoring that without sustained demand signals and cross-administration policy certainty, long-lead infrastructure investment stalls.
insights.speaker: Lee Beck
insights.so_what: A hard number on policy-uncertainty damage; the $35bn of cancellations is a marker for how fragile the US clean-energy capex pipeline is to political swings.
insights.quote: In 2025 alone, businesses abandoned over $35 billion in clean energy projects.
insights.locator: If the US wants to remain competitive

insights.claim: Energy demand growth is structural and here to stay beyond AI data centers -- driven by heating, buildings, industrial electrification, and transport -- so it will persist even if AI data-center buildout pace disappoints.
insights.speaker: Eric Rubenstein
insights.so_what: De-risks the electricity-demand thesis from AI hype: even bears on data centers should expect sustained load growth, supporting generation and grid investment.
insights.quote: Even as analysts debate the pace and scale of AI data center buildout, energy demand growth is here to stay
insights.locator: The decisions driving energy innovation

insights.claim: Elevated oil prices could strengthen EV demand, which Kurt Kelty of General Motors argues is the demand signal needed to catalyze US energy innovation.
insights.speaker: Lee Beck
insights.so_what: Higher-for-longer oil is a second-order tailwind for EV demand and the US innovation flywheel; watch oil price as an EV adoption catalyst.
insights.locator: Higher energy prices may further accelerate

themes.theme: China's clean-tech dominance and nuclear design lock-in

insights.claim: 94 percent of nuclear reactors that began construction over the past decade were based on Russian or Chinese designs, ceding the global nuclear newbuild standard to those two powers.
insights.speaker: Lee Beck
insights.so_what: A geopolitical lock-in metric: 94% Russian/Chinese reactor designs means decades of dependence and influence -- a structural gap for Western SMR and reactor vendors to close.
insights.quote: 94 percent of nuclear reactors that began construction over the past decade were based on Russian or Chinese designs
insights.locator: More must be done to support innovators

insights.claim: China controls over 80 percent of the solar value chain and manufactures 80 percent of battery supply, and directs nearly half of its clean-tech exports to emerging markets.
insights.speaker: Lee Beck
insights.so_what: Quantifies Chinese chokehold on solar and batteries; supply-chain concentration is both a cost advantage for China and a strategic vulnerability/onshoring opportunity for the West.
insights.quote: China's share in the solar value chain exceeds 80 percent, it also manufactures 80 percent of battery supply, and directs nearly half of its clean tech exports to emerging markets
insights.locator: More must be done to support innovators
